frames.caption: Plan: dp[i] = max money robbable from houses 0..i. Sweep left to right; at each house pick the better of skip vs rob.
frames.note: House Robber is the archetypal "decision per cell" 1D DP. State: dp[i] = the most money robbable from houses 0..i with no two adjacent robbed. The only freedom at house i is binary — skip it or rob it — and each choice points at an already-solved subproblem, so a single left-to-right sweep with O(1) memory of the last two answers solves it.

frames.caption: House 0 (value 2): two choices — SKIP keeps dp[-1]=0; ROB takes dp[-2]+2=0+2=2.
frames.note: House 0 has no earlier houses: skipping leaves dp[-1]=0 and robbing gives dp[-2]+nums[0]=0+2=2. The base cases are free because an empty prefix robs nothing.

frames.caption: dp[0] = max(0, 2) = 2. Rob wins; roll prev2←prev1, prev1←2.
frames.note: dp[i] is the larger of the two branches, so each cell locks in the optimum for its prefix and later cells reuse it without recomputation. Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], the array collapses to two rolling variables — that is the reviewer's prev2/prev1 and the O(1)-space version of this exact recurrence.

frames.caption: House 1 (value 7): two choices — SKIP keeps dp[0]=2; ROB takes dp[-1]+7=0+7=7.
frames.note: The ROB branch is dp[i-2] + nums[i]: taking house i forbids its neighbor i-1, so we must build on dp[i-2] (the best that already excludes house i-1) and never dp[i-1]. Reaching back two cells is exactly what enforces the no-two-adjacent rule — it is the whole trick of this DP.

frames.caption: dp[1] = max(2, 7) = 7. Rob wins; roll prev2←prev1, prev1←7.
frames.note: dp[i] is the larger of the two branches, so each cell locks in the optimum for its prefix and later cells reuse it without recomputation. Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], the array collapses to two rolling variables — that is the reviewer's prev2/prev1 and the O(1)-space version of this exact recurrence.

frames.caption: House 2 (value 9): two choices — SKIP keeps dp[1]=7; ROB takes dp[0]+9=2+9=11.
frames.note: The ROB branch is dp[i-2] + nums[i]: taking house i forbids its neighbor i-1, so we must build on dp[i-2] (the best that already excludes house i-1) and never dp[i-1]. Reaching back two cells is exactly what enforces the no-two-adjacent rule — it is the whole trick of this DP.

frames.caption: dp[2] = max(7, 11) = 11. Rob wins; roll prev2←prev1, prev1←11.
frames.note: dp[i] is the larger of the two branches, so each cell locks in the optimum for its prefix and later cells reuse it without recomputation. Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], the array collapses to two rolling variables — that is the reviewer's prev2/prev1 and the O(1)-space version of this exact recurrence.

frames.caption: House 3 (value 3): two choices — SKIP keeps dp[2]=11; ROB takes dp[1]+3=7+3=10.
frames.note: The SKIP branch carries dp[i-1] forward unchanged: if we do not rob house i, the best we can do over 0..i is exactly the best over 0..i-1. It is the "do nothing new" option and it is always legal — robbing the previous house is fine when we leave this one alone.

frames.caption: dp[3] = max(11, 10) = 11. Skip wins; roll prev2←prev1, prev1←11.
frames.note: dp[i] is the larger of the two branches, so each cell locks in the optimum for its prefix and later cells reuse it without recomputation. Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], the array collapses to two rolling variables — that is the reviewer's prev2/prev1 and the O(1)-space version of this exact recurrence.

frames.caption: House 4 (value 1): two choices — SKIP keeps dp[3]=11; ROB takes dp[2]+1=11+1=12.
frames.note: The ROB branch is dp[i-2] + nums[i]: taking house i forbids its neighbor i-1, so we must build on dp[i-2] (the best that already excludes house i-1) and never dp[i-1]. Reaching back two cells is exactly what enforces the no-two-adjacent rule — it is the whole trick of this DP.

frames.caption: dp[4] = max(11, 12) = 12. Rob wins; roll prev2←prev1, prev1←12.
frames.note: dp[i] is the larger of the two branches, so each cell locks in the optimum for its prefix and later cells reuse it without recomputation. Because dp[i] only ever reads dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], the array collapses to two rolling variables — that is the reviewer's prev2/prev1 and the O(1)-space version of this exact recurrence.

frames.caption: Answer = dp[4] = 12. The optimal subset is houses 0, 2, 4 = 2 + 9 + 1 = 12.
frames.note: The last cell is the answer over all houses. Every cell was computed once from two earlier cells, so the algorithm is O(n) time, and because only the previous two values are ever read it is O(1) space — the two rolling variables prev2/prev1. This skip-or-take-with-a-two-back-reach skeleton recurs across many DPs (Climbing Stairs, Decode Ways, and House Robber II, which just runs this line twice on a circle).
